title: How Long Will They Keep Deceiving This Country!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored; the opening newspaper headline, power-purchase rate, tender dates and project figures have been checked against the printed pages; and clear OCR character and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: On 29 November 2014, Tamil Nadu's Electricity Minister issued a lengthy statement about power generation in the State and when newly launched projects would begin production. The very headline given to that statement by the newspapers was: “Rs 10,121-crore Ennore power project to begin generation in four years; order to be issued shortly”!

At a time when the Tamil Nadu Electricity Regulatory Commission had levelled numerous serious charges against the Electricity Board, the Minister did not answer them directly. Instead, on 19 November 2014, he issued a statement that appeared to assail the Regulatory Commission itself. Industrialists and social activists immediately voiced strong objections, asking, “Is the Minister issuing orders to the Electricity Regulatory Commission?”

How many assurances about power generation and power cuts did former Chief Minister Jayalalithaa and the Minister for the department give in the Assembly? How many? But what is the position today? On 29 November 2014, a newspaper carried the headline “Electricity Board reluctant to announce power cuts officially; homes and industrial establishments affected by sudden outages.” It reported: “Tamil Nadu's demand for electricity exceeds generation. To manage the shortage, a forty per cent power restriction was imposed on factories. Between 8 a.m. and 6 p.m., rotating cuts of two hours in Chennai and four hours elsewhere were imposed on residential consumers.”

Another report said: “Power cuts even during severe cold weather have caused deep public dissatisfaction in Tamil Nadu. Commercial generation at the third units in Thoothukudi and Vallur should have begun last month. Since production has still not started, Tamil Nadu has been denied 500 MW. The State is receiving reduced allocations from Central generating stations such as NLC and Simhadri. Electricity Board officials have therefore decided to have the Tamil Nadu Government write to the Union Government seeking a solution.”

Meanwhile, Tamil Nadu Congress president E.V.K.S. Elangovan told reporters: “There has been corruption in the purchase of electricity from private companies. Chief Minister Panneerselvam must investigate it and remove Electricity Minister Natham Viswanathan.” He asked further: “When electricity is purchased from public-sector undertakings such as Neyveli and Kudankulam at Rs 3 a unit, why is Rs 15.14 a unit paid for electricity bought from private companies? Enormous corruption has occurred in purchases by Tamil Nadu's Electricity Department.”

Further evidence supports this charge. For the current financial year, the Electricity Board estimated Rs 27,147 crore as the cost of purchasing power. Within that amount, a higher tariff was fixed exclusively for four specified private power stations than for electricity bought from Central stations, wind and solar plants, or out-of-State electricity traders. The Board's procurement costs have consequently multiplied.

It planned to purchase from the Central generating stations at Neyveli, Kalpakkam and Vallur, NTPC's Ramagundam station and the Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant at average rates of Rs 2.94 and Rs 3.85 a unit. Electricity from private wind, solar and biomass plants was bought at Rs 4.26, and from private electricity traders and captive private generators at Rs 4.16. Yet electricity from four private generators, Samalpatti, Madurai Power Corporation, Pillaiperumalnallur and GMR, was purchased at rates reaching Rs 15.14 a unit.

Only 798 million units were bought from those four companies for a total of Rs 1,208 crore. Electricity was bought from ST-CMS, Lanco and Pioneer at Rs 5.14 a unit. An estimated 2,865 million units were to be purchased from those companies for Rs 1,473 crore. The Electricity Board informed the Regulatory Commission that it would purchase a total of 65,870 million units during the current financial year for Rs 27,147 crore and Rs 5 lakh. Losses continue to rise because around fifty-five per cent of the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board's entire revenue is spent merely on buying power.

To buy electricity from private firms, the Board must formally obtain the Regulatory Commission's approval and fix a reasonable tariff. The Commission had warned that losses would increase if electricity was bought from private firms for more than Rs 5.50 a unit. More than that, its order dated 30 March 2012 said electricity should not be purchased from these four private companies. Yet for the past two years, even without any urgent necessity, power has been bought from those four companies at rates reaching Rs 15 a unit. Of approximately Rs 22,090 crore spent on electricity between April 2013 and March 2014, Rs 16,280 crore was spent on purchases from private companies alone.

Why does the Board, which buys electricity cheaply from everyone else, pay these four firms alone a higher price? The Times of India had already written on 10 August 2014: “Although Tamil Nadu generates large quantities of wind power, the Jayalalithaa Government does not purchase it and instead buys higher-priced electricity from private suppliers outside the State.” On 20 August 2014, a Tamil daily explained the issue under the headline “Tamil Nadu Electricity Board's true colours exposed: power cuts continue despite high-priced purchases.”

Though complaints have therefore been made repeatedly against this Government, neither the Tamil Nadu Government, the Chief Minister nor the Minister concerned has offered any answer. Does their refusal to answer not itself confirm that something improper is happening?

It is also alleged that the Electricity Board failed to submit its annual revenue and expenditure requirement properly to the Regulatory Commission or place it before the public. A public-interest petition was filed in the Madras High Court, notices were issued to the Board and the Commission, and the hearing was adjourned to 16 December. The electricity-tariff increase has not been implemented because of that case, public opposition to the increase and the approaching Srirangam by-election. Officials say the Board's financial requirement for the current year will be Rs 39,092 crore, while tariff collection will produce only Rs 32,238 crore, leaving a revenue deficit of Rs 6,854 crore.

In his statement of the twenty-ninth, the Minister says: “It is noteworthy that this Government has systematically taken many measures to make Tamil Nadu self-sufficient in power generation.” Explaining those measures, he cites the 660-MW Ennore Thermal Power Project expansion and says it will begin generation after forty-two months, in 2018. He conceals the fact that the project was started under the DMK Government and then neglected for years under AIADMK rule.

In a statement read under Rule 110 in the Assembly on 29 March 2012, former Chief Minister Jayalalithaa announced that the Ennore Thermal Power expansion would begin generation by the end of 2015. Jayalalithaa said 2015; the Minister now says it is expected to begin in 2018. Does that not show precisely how vigorously the AIADMK Government is pursuing self-sufficiency in power generation?

That is not all. The Minister's statement says that the construction order for the Udangudi Thermal Power Project will be issued shortly and that generation is expected to begin in 2018-19. In its Budget presented on 4 August 2011, the AIADMK Government announced that power schemes such as the Udangudi expansion would be launched soon.

Then, in her Rule 110 statement of 29 March 2012, Jayalalithaa said that a feasibility report was being prepared as the first step towards launching the 800-MW Udangudi expansion thermal project and that it would become operational in the 2015-16 financial year. Again, on 25 April 2013, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa read another Rule 110 statement in the Assembly: “International tenders have been received and are under scrutiny for the 660-MW Ennore expansion project. Tenders have also been called for the 2 x 660-MW Ennore Special Economic Zone project and the 2 x 660-MW Udangudi Thermal Power Project.”

But the Electricity Minister's statement of 29 November 2014 says: “The engineering, procurement and construction order for establishing the Udangudi Thermal Power Project will be issued shortly; generation is expected to begin in 2018-19.” What, then, is the truth?

The Electricity Board's Chief Engineer for Projects prepared and sent notes on power projects for the forthcoming Assembly session. On Udangudi, he wrote that the technical bids were opened on 19 July 2013 and the price bids on 18 October 2014, and that they remained under scrutiny. Regarding the Udangudi expansion, he wrote: “The pre-feasibility report is being prepared. The Union Ministry of Environment and Forests will shortly be approached to finalise the terms of reference. The order for this project was issued on 4 September 2014. A request seeking the Tamil Nadu Government's in-principle approval was sent on 15 September 2014. The project is expected to become operational in 2019-20.”

It was the same with Jayalalithaa's Rule 110 statement of 25 April 2013, in which she said: “The 2,000-MW Sillahalla pumped-storage hydroelectric project will be developed in the Nilgiris district at an estimated cost of Rs 7,000 crore.” Her party members immediately applauded. Eighteen months have passed since she read it. Yet the Chief Engineer's report states: “In-principle approval for this project was granted in Tamil Nadu Government Order No. 87 dated 11 September 2014.”

Can we not understand from this what dramas of deception this Government stages over power generation? Thinking of all this suffering brings to mind the song from *Malaikkallan*, for which I wrote the screenplay and dialogue and in which my dear friend M.G.R. acted:
“How long will he deceive this country?
How much longer will he deceive this country,
His own country, our country?
He acts like a noble man who never strays from truth,
And, watching for his chance, loots in many ways!
Wearing a daylight disguise like a devotee,
He traps ordinary people in his net.
How long will he deceive this country?”
That is the song I feel compelled to remember!
With affection,
M.K.
2 December 2014
